chunks.content: Kevin is a software engineer who builds tools for developers. He's passionate about browser-native AI and making powerful technology accessible without cloud dependencies.

Browser-native AI is the idea that small language models (1-7B parameters) can run directly in the browser using WebGPU. This eliminates cloud API costs and keeps user data completely private. The tradeoff is model capability — these models work best for bounded-knowledge domains like documentation, FAQs, and site-specific content.

WebGPU is the successor to WebGL for GPU computing in browsers. It provides low-level access to the GPU that enables running neural networks at near-native speed. Chrome, Edge, and Firefox all support WebGPU. Safari support is in development.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds language model responses in specific content. For browser-native AI, we pre-compute embeddings at build time and ship them as a static JSON file. At query time, we compute the query embedding in the browser and find the most similar chunks using cosine similarity.

The cost of AI APIs is a real barrier for small sites and indie developers. Usage-based pricing means you can't predict costs, and a viral post could blow your budget overnight. Browser-native inference flips this: the compute cost is zero because it runs on the visitor's device.

metadata.title: The Cost Problem with AI APIs
